pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Broad eval runs increased the number of scored cases but made it harder for reviewers to distinguish repeated failure patterns from one-off mistakes; smaller batches with visible clusters produced more actionable changes.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly says the broad run increased scored cases but reviewers could not distinguish repeated patterns from one-offs, while the smaller batch grouped citation issues and produced more actionable prompt changes. S5 reinforces that ungrouped failures slowed review and reviewers preferred fewer clearer cases.

claimJudgments.claimText: Coverage can improve while grounding/citation support regresses, so citation support should be an explicit release/shipping gate rather than a leaderboard target.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 explicitly states coverage can improve while grounding regresses and that citation support needs a shipping gate, not a leaderboard target. S6 also asks to hold citation support as a shipping gate.

claimJudgments.claimText: Long traces are useful for debugging, but unchecked retries caused a 1.34x cost increase; finance will accept trace retention if retries are capped and summarized in a run manifest (retry count, cost ratio, and what the trace resolved).
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly supports all elements: long traces helped debug/diagnose failures, unchecked retries caused a 1.34x cost increase, and finance accepted trace retention if retries are capped and the manifest includes retry count, cost ratio, and an explanation of what the trace helped resolve.

claimJudgments.claimText: Holdout cases should be visible only as summaries/aggregate scores in dashboards; labels/expected answers should be excluded from prompt-iteration workflows to reduce memorization/overfitting risk.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 says holdout cases should appear in dashboards as summaries and aggregate scores, while tuning labels/expected answers stay out of prompt-iteration workflows to avoid memorizing visible examples. S6 also says holdout performance should be reported only in aggregate.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers want dashboards that separately cluster unsupported claims, weak citations, cost regressions, and holdout concerns; they prefer fewer cases with clearer evidence over large runs requiring manual interpretation per row.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 directly says reviewers asked for a dashboard clustering unsupported claims, weak citations, cost regressions, and holdout concerns separately, and preferred fewer cases with clearer evidence over larger runs where every row required manual interpretation.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well supported by the packet: S6 recommends one more visible batch and allows expansion only if grounding improves without exceeding the cost guardrail; S1 and S5 support smaller clustered batches before broader coverage; S2 supports a citation-support shipping gate; S3 supports retaining traces with retry/cost controls; and S4 supports aggregate-only holdout visibility. The briefing appropriately balances coverage, citation support, trace utility, and cost, and its open questions acknowledge unspecified thresholds rather than inventing them.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
